content.full_text:
California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornrnission
August L7, L99O
Christopher Lamerdin
lIurray and Associates
17 81 Union street
San Francisco, CA 94]-23-4426
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our FiIe No. f-9 0-4I2
Dear !,tr . Larnerdin:
This is in response to your request for assistance regarding
the application of the canpaign provisions of the Po1itical Reforrn
Act (the "Actu1.r7 Since your request is general in nature and
does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we.are
treating your request as one for inforural assistance.2/
OUESTION
Is the attached memorandum discussing the contrj.bution liroits
of the Act accurate?
CONCLUSION
The memorandun that you have encLosed with your advice
request which discusses the contribution linitations 
of the Act is
substantially correct. 
However, rre would suggest that you
incorporate the additionat inforrnation as discussed in this letter
to clarify other aspects of the contribution linits of the Act.
DTSCUSSTON
Proposition 73 was adopted by the voters in the June 1988
Prinary alection. This new 1aw imposed a number of restrictions
7/ Government code Sections 81ooo-91015. AlI statutory refer-
ences are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. com-
nission regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations
Section 18OOO, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the california code of Requlations.
2/ rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnrnunity provided by an opinion or formal rrritten advice. (Govern-
ment code Section 83114i Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 807 . Sacramento cA 95804.0807 . (916) 322-5660


Our File No. I-9O-4A2
Page 2
on candidates and officeholders regarding the collection and
expenditure of campaign funds. (section 85100, et seo. ) As is
accurately reflected in your memorandum, ttre Act, as anended by
eroposition 73, now provides that contributions to candidates for
elettive office and political 
committees must comply with the
contribution linits set forth in sections 85301, 85302 85303 and
85303.3/ contributions from a person to a candidate are lirnited
to 91,ooo in any fiscat year-11 (Section 85301(a).) contributions
fron a political 
committee to a candidate are linited to $2,500
each f i,sca1 year, and contributions from a broad based political
cornrnittee or political party to a candidate are linited to $5,000
per fiscal year. (Section 85303.)
Contributions to a political 
committee or broad based
political 
courmittee are finited to $2,500 per fiscal year Per
Lontributor. 
(Section 85302.) where contributions are Dade to a
committee for purposes other than contributions to candidates, the
contributions are not sub'iect to the linits of the Act.
(Regulation 18535, copy eiclosed. ) 5/
You also note that the definition of trPerson'r in Section
85102(b) of the Act includes individuals, proprietorships, firns,
partnerships, joint ventures, syndicates, business trusts,
-ompanies, corporations, associations, committees, and labor
orginizations. 
Thus, every corporation is a person within the
meaning of Section 85102 (b) .
However, you may also wish to note that under some
circumstances the Act requires that contributions made by more
than one person be cumulated and the persons be treated as a
single contributor to determine if the persons have reached the
contribution linits of the Act.
cumulation is required under tuo lines of authority. 
First,
ii !976, the Cornmission set out standards for the cumulation of
contributions in two opinions, fn re Kahn (1976) 2 FPPC ops. 150
and fn re Lumsdon (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 140 (copj.es enclosed). In
3/ 
Section 85305 sets separate contributi'on lirnits for special
elections.
4/ The fiscal year is JuIy 1 through June 30. (section 85102(a).)
5/ rn the first 
sentence of section three of your menorandum you
appropriately state that where a person contributes to a comrnittee
and earmarks the contribution for specific candidates, the
committee becones a nere intermediary and the contribution is
subject to the $1,ooo contribution lirnit. 
Both internedj.ary (the
connittee) and the contributor nust be discLosed to the recipient.
In addition, the fourth sentence of section three of your
memorandum is unclear as to rrhether the $2,500 lirnit applies to
contributions to committees for purposes other than naking
contributions to candidates.


Our FiIe No. I-9O-4A2
Page 3
the Kahn opinion the commission concluded that cumulation of
contributions was required where contributions were made by a
parent company and its lrholly owned subsidiary. In the Lumsdon
opinion, the conmission found cumulation of contributions was
required where contributions were made by a corporation and the
corporation's rnajority shareholder. Both the opinions focused on
the ability of one Person to control the contributions of anottrer.
At the Cornmission's June 1989 neeting, Regulation 18531.5
(copy enclosed) was adopted to further clarify when cumulation is
appropriate,S/ Specifically, where the question concerns two
contributors which are both entities, ReguJ-ation 18531.5 requires
curuulation under the following circumstances:
(a) If the same person or a majority of the
same persons in fact directs and controls the deci-
sions of two or nore entities to nake contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose a candidate or
candidates for elective office, those affiliated
entities shalI be considered one person, one
pol.itical conmittee, or one broad based political
tommittee for purposes of the contribution linita-
tions in Government Code Sections 85301, a53o2,
85303 and 85305.
(b) Business entities in a parent-subsidiary
relationship and business entities with the same
controlling (more than 5o-percent) owner shaI1 be
considered one person for purposes of the contribu-
tion limitations in Government code sections 85301,
a53o2, 85303 and 85305, unless the business enti-
ties act conpletely independently in their deci-
sions to make contributions and expenditures to
support or oppose candidates for elective office.
Foi purposes of this section, a parent-subsidiary
relationship exists when one business entity owns
more than 50 percent of another business entity.
Thus, the reg"ulation requires cumulation of contributions
nade by two different entities where: (1) the sane person or a
ma'ioritv of the same persons, (2) in fact directs and controls,
(3) the decisions of two or nore entities, 
(4) to nake
contributions or expenditures to supoort or oppose a candidate or
candidates for elective office. 
If there is no direction and
control in fact by the same person or najority of Persons as to
either entity, cumulation is not required.
6/ Regulation 18531.5 has been subnitted to the office of
Adninistrative Law for review. Although not currently effective,
the regulation expresses the Commissj.on's policy concerning
cunulation of contributions.


Our Fi■e No.  ■-90-482
Page 4
et al. v.
′ UoS. District
Court, Eastern District of Ca■
ifornia′  Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK―
JFM, the Plaintiffs 
are chaLlenging the Act's contribution linits
as they apply to candidates and political 
parties. 
fn TaxPavers
to Lirnit Canpaign v. r'air Politica1 Practices Conmission,
California Supreme court, case No. 5012016, the Plaintiffs 
assert
that certain contribution linitation 
provisions of Proposition 68
(a1so passed at the June 7, 1988 Primary election) must be
enforced in addition to the provj.sions of Proposition 73.
Fina Ily ,
pending which
linits 
under
ff you have
pLease feel free
SH:」WW:dg
Enc10sures
please note that there are two lawsuits currently
nay significantly affect the current contribution
the Act.  In
any further questj.ons regarding this matter,
to contact ne at (916) 322-590]-.
Sincere■y′
Scott Hal■abrin
w. wallace
1, Legal Divi,sion
